context.project_description: Add token usage tracking for LLM queries across all supported providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) to enable users to monitor LLM cost consumption by logging input and output tokens per chat operation.
key_requirements: Implement token counting capability at LLM provider level for all current providers
Track both input and output tokens where available for each chat operation
Integrate with existing instrumentation/benchmarking hooks without breaking changes
Log token usage per query with provider identification for visibility
constraints: Must work with existing LLM provider architecture (OpenAIChat, AnthropicChat, etc.)
Cannot change public interfaces of any provider classes
Must be backward compatible - no breaking changes to current API
Should integrate cleanly with current benchmarking/instrumentation system
context.existing_context: fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py, fitz_ai/llm/providers/openai.py, fitz_ai/llm/providers/anthropic.py, fitz_ai/llm/providers/cohere.py, fitz_ai/llm/providers/ollama.py, fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py, fitz_ai/logging/logger.py
stakeholders: Developer who needs to know when queries are expensive for debugging and optimization
DevOps/Platform Team that wants to monitor overall resource utilization and costs across their LLM infrastructure
Product Manager interested in understanding query cost patterns to inform pricing or budgeting decisions

in_scope: Basic token counting capability at LLM provider level
Support for all current providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama)
Simple logging of token usage per chat operation
out_of_scope: Cost calculation based on pricing tiers
Storage of historical token usage data

existing_files: fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py — Provider protocols for LLM clients
fitz_ai/llm/providers/openai.py — OpenAI provider wrappers using the official SDK
fitz_ai/llm/providers/anthropic.py — Anthropic provider wrappers using the official SDK
fitz_ai/llm/providers/cohere.py — Cohere provider wrappers using the official SDK
fitz_ai/llm/providers/ollama.py — Ollama provider wrappers using direct HTTP calls
fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py — Instrumentation system for benchmarking plugin performance
fitz_ai/logging/logger.py — Unified logging setup for the entire Fitz project
needed_artifacts: fitz_ai/llm/token_counter.py — Token counting logic and provider-specific implementations
fitz_ai/core/metrics/token_usage.py — Data structures for representing token usage metrics (removed due to scope inflation)

assumptions.assumption: Token counting is per-provider specific (OpenAI vs Cohere uses different tokenizers)
assumptions.impact: Would need a universal tokenizer, possibly with poor accuracy if incorrect

assumptions.assumption: The tracking should happen at the provider level, not in engine or SDK layers
assumptions.impact: Would require more intrusive changes to engine layers if wrong

assumptions.assumption: The system currently doesn't track tokens anywhere - this is a new capability
assumptions.impact: May conflict with existing systems or logging patterns if tracking already occurs elsewhere

assumptions.assumption: Users want to know costs for both input and output tokens (if available)
assumptions.impact: Tracking only one type would provide incomplete cost visibility for users

assumptions.assumption: Current benchmark system is the right way to surface this data
assumptions.impact: Would need alternative reporting mechanism like metrics export if incorrect

assumptions.assumption: Token usage only needed for chat operations
assumptions.impact: Would also need to track embedding/rerank token usage if wrong, expanding scope significantly

approaches.name: Provider-Specific Instrumentation with Manual Hook Integration
approaches.description: Adding token counting logic directly into each LLM provider class by modifying their chat, embed, and related methods to parse or extract token usage from API responses.
pros: Each provider knows its own response format
cons: High operational burden due to manual modification of every provider class
Error-prone duplication across providers with different API structures
best_for: Small number of providers with consistent API responses

approaches.name: Shared Token Counter Class with Protocol Extension
approaches.description: Introducing a shared abstraction for token parsing by defining a new protocol (TokenProvider) and creating a centralized TokenCounter utility class that encapsulates token extraction logic per provider type.
pros: Centralized logic reduces duplication across providers
Encapsulates provider-specific parsing in one place
cons: Requires implementing new protocols and utilities not yet present in codebase
Still requires individual updates to all existing providers for token extraction
best_for: When a shared abstraction is desired but no existing infrastructure supports it

approaches.name: Instrumentation-Based Hook Injection (Recommended)
approaches.description: Leveraging the existing InstrumentedProxy and hooks infrastructure to inject token tracking into LLM method calls via a new hook class, allowing capturing usage without altering provider interfaces directly.
pros: Keeps token counting logic decoupled from business logic in providers
Allows flexible opt-in behavior for instrumentation
cons: Providers must go through the instrumentation system to capture tokens
Some APIs (Anthropic, Ollama) do not natively expose token usage information
best_for: When minimizing changes to provider interfaces is important

architecture.reasoning: Approach C is recommended because it reuses existing observability infrastructure without introducing breaking changes. It keeps token counting logic decoupled from business logic, promoting modularity and clean separation of concerns. Additionally, it allows for flexible opt-in behavior where instrumentation can be enabled/disabled per usage context or globally via configuration. Approach A fails due to duplication across providers leading to inconsistency and tight coupling; Approach B also duplicates work since parsing still needs to happen on a per-provider basis if not properly abstracted in shared utilities that don't exist yet.

key_tradeoffs.provider_specific_vs_shared_logic: Approach A (provider-specific) simplifies per-provider logic but increases maintenance burden and inconsistency; Approach B (shared) centralizes logic but requires careful abstraction since no shared implementation exists yet. Approach C (instrumentation-based) offers the best balance by reusing existing infrastructure with minimal changes.
key_tradeoffs.instrumentation_enforcement: Enforcing all providers through InstrumentedProxy adds consistency but introduces risk if some calls bypass instrumentation, leading to missing telemetry data.

technology_considerations: InstrumentedProxy and hooks system in fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py for reusable observability infrastructure
TokenCounter utility class in fitz_ai/llm/token_counter.py to encapsulate provider-specific token parsing logic
ChatProvider and EmbeddingProvider protocols as base interfaces that need minimal extension for token tracking
OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama API response formats with varying usage field structures requiring distinct extraction methods
architecture.scope_statement: Implement token tracking using existing InstrumentedProxy hooks with new TokenTrackingHook, adding fitz_ai/llm/token_counter.py module for provider-specific response parsing logic.

adrs.title: ADR: Instrumentation-Based Token Tracking for LLM Providers
adrs.context: The need to track token usage across different LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) while maintaining clean separation of concerns and leveraging existing observability infrastructure.
adrs.decision: Adopt Approach C: Instrumentation-Based Hook Injection using the existing BenchmarkHook system in fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py to capture token usage without modifying provider interfaces directly.
adrs.rationale: This approach reuses existing infrastructure, keeps business logic clean from telemetry concerns, allows flexible opt-in behavior, and is easily extensible for new providers. It avoids tight coupling and duplication inherent in approaches A and B.
consequences: Low operational burden as it leverages existing systems
Token counting can be enabled/disabled per context or globally via configuration
Providers currently instantiated directly will need to go through instrumentation system for token tracking (currently not enforced)
Only providers that are instrumented will have tokens tracked; direct method calls bypass this logic
alternatives_considered: Approach A – Provider-Specific Instrumentation with Manual Hook Integration: rejected because it duplicates logic across providers leading to inconsistency and tight coupling

Approach B – Shared Token Counter Class with Protocol Extension: rejected because the required protocol (TokenProvider) and shared implementation don't exist yet, and would still require per-provider modification

adrs.title: ADR: Centralized Instrumentation Data Collection vs. Provider-Specific Metrics
adrs.context: The instrumentation-based hook injection approach centralizes token counting and usage metrics collection through a shared observability infrastructure rather than having each provider maintain its own metrics.
adrs.decision: Adopt centralized instrumentation data collection with cross-provider aggregation capabilities
adrs.rationale: This enables unified monitoring, consistent billing calculations across providers, and reduces duplication of metric collection logic. It supports the flexible opt-in behavior while maintaining a single source of truth for usage analytics.
consequences: Cross-cutting concern: Token data now flows through shared instrumentation layers instead of being contained within individual provider components
Increased complexity in failure handling - if central instrumentation fails, all providers may lose token metrics
Potential privacy implications when aggregating usage data from different providers into single telemetry streams
alternatives_considered: Provider-specific instrumentation with separate metric collection for each component (Approach A)
Hybrid approach where critical metrics are collected locally and aggregated periodically via batch jobs

artifacts.content:
# Copyright (c) 2024 Fitz AI Inc.

"""
Token counting utilities for LLM providers.

This module provides logic to extract token usage information from responses
returned by various LLM APIs. Each provider's response parsing is encapsulated here,
so that the instrumentation system can consistently log tokens without needing
provider-specific code.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

class TokenCounter:
    """
    Utility class for extracting token usage from various LLM API responses.
    """

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_openai_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Extract input/output tokens from OpenAI-style response data.

        Example response format (from chat/completion):
        {
          "usage": {
            "prompt_tokens": 10,
            "completion_tokens": 25,
            "total_tokens": 35
          }
        }

        Returns:
            Tuple of (input_tokens, output_tokens) or None if not found.
        """
        usage = response_data.get("usage")
        if not isinstance(usage, dict):
            return None
        
        prompt_tokens = usage.get("prompt_tokens", 0)
        completion_tokens = usage.get("completion_tokens", 0)
        return (prompt_tokens, completion_tokens)

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_anthropic_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Extract input/output tokens from Anthropic-style response data.

        Note that Anthropic does not provide built-in token counting in its API responses,
        so we return None here to indicate this should be handled separately (e.g., by a client-side counter).

        Returns:
            Always returns None as Anthropic doesn't provide usage fields natively.
        """
        # TODO: Implement if Anthropic adds token tracking in SDK response
        return None

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_cohere_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Extract input/output tokens from Cohere-style response data.

        Example response format (from chat/completion):
        {
          "meta": {
            "tokens": {
              "prompt_tokens": 12,
              "response_tokens": 30
            }
          }
        }

        Returns:
            Tuple of (input_tokens, output_tokens) or None if not found.
        """
        meta = response_data.get("meta", {})
        tokens = meta.get("tokens", {})
        
        prompt_tokens = tokens.get("prompt_tokens", 0)
        response_tokens = tokens.get("response_tokens", 0)
        return (prompt_tokens, response_tokens)

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_ollama_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Extract input/output tokens from Ollama-style response data.

        Note that Ollama does not expose token usage via its API responses;
        this method always returns None.

        Returns:
            Always returns None as Ollama doesn't provide usage fields natively.
        """
        # TODO: Future work may parse Ollama logs or metrics if supported in future versions
        return None

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_default_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Fallback method for unknown APIs.

        Returns:
            Always returns None to avoid errors on unrecognized API formats.
        """
        return None

__all__ = ["TokenCounter"]
artifacts.purpose: Provides utility functions to extract token usage information from various LLM provider responses.

consequences: Low operational burden as it leverages existing systems
Token counting can be enabled/disabled per context or globally via configuration
Providers currently instantiated directly will need to go through instrumentation system for token tracking (currently not enforced)
Only providers that are instrumented will have tokens tracked; direct method calls bypass this logic

Approach B – Shared Token Counter Class with Protocol Extension: rejected because the required protocol (TokenProvider) and shared implementation don't exist yet, and would still require per-provider modification

adrs.context: The instrumentation-based hook injection approach centralizes token counting and usage metrics collection through a shared observability infrastructure rather than having each provider maintain its own metrics.
adrs.decision: Adopt centralized instrumentation data collection with cross-provider aggregation capabilities
adrs.rationale: This enables unified monitoring, consistent billing calculations across providers, and reduces duplication of metric collection logic. It supports the flexible opt-in behavior while maintaining a single source of truth for usage analytics.
consequences: Cross-cutting concern: Token data now flows through shared instrumentation layers instead of being contained within individual provider components
Increased complexity in failure handling - if central instrumentation fails, all providers may lose token metrics
Potential privacy implications when aggregating usage data from different providers into single telemetry streams
alternatives_considered: Provider-specific instrumentation with separate metric collection for each component (Approach A)
Hybrid approach where critical metrics are collected locally and aggregated periodically via batch jobs

# Copyright (c) 2024 Fitz AI Inc.

"""
Token counting utilities for LLM providers.

This module provides logic to extract token usage information from responses
returned by various LLM APIs. Each provider's response parsing is encapsulated here,
so that the instrumentation system can consistently log tokens without needing
provider-specific code.

from __future__ import annotations

class TokenCounter:
    """
    Utility class for extracting token usage from various LLM API responses.
    """

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_openai_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Extract input/output tokens from OpenAI-style response data.

        Example response format (from chat/completion):
        {
          "usage": {
            "prompt_tokens": 10,
            "completion_tokens": 25,
            "total_tokens": 35
          }
        }

        Returns:
            Tuple of (input_tokens, output_tokens) or None if not found.
        """
        usage = response_data.get("usage")
        if not isinstance(usage, dict):
            return None
        
        prompt_tokens = usage.get("prompt_tokens", 0)
        completion_tokens = usage.get("completion_tokens", 0)
        return (prompt_tokens, completion_tokens)

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_anthropic_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Extract input/output tokens from Anthropic-style response data.

        Note that Anthropic does not provide built-in token counting in its API responses,
        so we return None here to indicate this should be handled separately (e.g., by a client-side counter).

        Returns:
            Always returns None as Anthropic doesn't provide usage fields natively.
        """
        # TODO: Implement if Anthropic adds token tracking in SDK response
        return None

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_cohere_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Extract input/output tokens from Cohere-style response data.

        Example response format (from chat/completion):
        {
          "meta": {
            "tokens": {
              "prompt_tokens": 12,
              "response_tokens": 30
            }
          }
        }

        Returns:
            Tuple of (input_tokens, output_tokens) or None if not found.
        """
        meta = response_data.get("meta", {})
        tokens = meta.get("tokens", {})
        
        prompt_tokens = tokens.get("prompt_tokens", 0)
        response_tokens = tokens.get("response_tokens", 0)
        return (prompt_tokens, response_tokens)

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_ollama_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Extract input/output tokens from Ollama-style response data.

        Note that Ollama does not expose token usage via its API responses;
        this method always returns None.

        Returns:
            Always returns None as Ollama doesn't provide usage fields natively.
        """
        # TODO: Future work may parse Ollama logs or metrics if supported in future versions
        return None

    @staticmethod
    def extract_tokens_from_default_response(response_data: dict) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
        """
        Fallback method for unknown APIs.

        Returns:
            Always returns None to avoid errors on unrecognized API formats.
        """
        return None

__all__ = ["TokenCounter"]
artifacts.purpose: Provides utility functions to extract token usage information from various LLM provider responses.

phases.name: Read Before Writing
phases.objective: Verify integration points by reading source

phases.name: Token Tracking Hook Design
phases.objective: Design a token tracking system using existing instrumentation framework
key_risks: Different LLM providers expose tokens in inconsistent ways
Parsing token usage from responses may require deep provider integration

phases.name: Provider Integration and Instrumentation
phases.objective: Integrate token tracking with all LLM providers using instrumentation system
deliverables: Instrumented OpenAIChat, AnthropicChat, CohereChat, OllamaChat objects
Token counting logic for each provider type
key_risks: Provider response format inconsistencies requiring custom parsing per provider
Performance overhead from token tracking instrumentation in production

phases.name: Structured Logging Integration
phases.objective: Integrate token usage data with existing structured logging system
deliverables: Contextual fields for query/session tracking of tokens
key_risks: Token data must be captured without interfering with existing log structure
Potential performance impact on logging overhead

risks.description: Different LLM providers expose token usage in inconsistent ways, requiring custom parsing logic per provider type.
risks.mitigation: Implement provider-specific token parsing handlers for OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, and Ollama with unit tests covering each response format.
risks.contingency: Fall back to approximate token estimation using text length if detailed usage metadata is not available from a provider.
risks.verification: Verify that each LLM provider's chat method returns structured data containing token usage information or implement fallback logic.

risks.description: Instrumentation system may miss some method calls due to dynamic proxy wrapping limitations.
risks.mitigation: Add comprehensive logging and assertions in the InstrumentedProxy class to ensure all tracked methods are properly wrapped and called.
risks.contingency: Manually verify that key LLM methods (chat, chat_stream) are instrumented by inspecting call traces during runtime testing.
risks.verification: Assert that the InstrumentedProxy correctly wraps all targeted method names in each provider class.

risks.description: Token tracking introduces additional computational overhead from parsing responses and maintaining state.
risks.mitigation: Implement efficient, lazy token counting only when instrumentation is enabled via configuration flags.
risks.contingency: Disable token tracking in production environments by default unless explicitly opted-in through environment variables or config.
risks.verification: Measure CPU/memory impact of token tracking using benchmark tests and assert that it remains below threshold.

risks.description: Token estimation accuracy may vary across different models due to differences in tokenization algorithms.
risks.mitigation: Use official tokenizer libraries provided by each LLM vendor when possible instead of heuristics based on text length.
risks.contingency: Provide configurable fallback behavior for unknown or unsupported providers to log approximate token counts with a warning message.
risks.verification: Compare actual vs estimated token counts using known reference texts and assert that variance stays within acceptable tolerance.

recommended_contingencies: Fall back to approximate token estimation using text length if detailed usage metadata is not available from a provider
Manually verify that key LLM methods (chat, chat_stream) are instrumented by inspecting call traces during runtime testing
Disable token tracking in production environments by default unless explicitly opted-in through environment variables or config
Provide configurable fallback behavior for unknown or unsupported providers to log approximate token counts with a warning message
